                                                                                                                                   Comment
                                                                                                                                   if 4D+ see Bulk (below)
                              SOPHONT SIZE
                                  Size is the volume of a sophont in liters (1000 liters to a      C2 Dexterity      Dice
                              cubic meter). Size is also approximate mass in kg.                   C2 Grace          Dice / 2      makes it lighter or smaller
                                                                                                   C2 Agility        Dice / 2      makes it lighter or smaller
                                  Size Benchmark is Human = 72                                     C3 Endurance      Dice
                                                                                                   C3 Stamina        Dice * 2      makes it heavier or larger
                                 Average Sophont Size is based on the three physical char-         C3 Vigor          Dice / 2      makes it lighter or smaller
                              acteristics C1 C2 C3 using the Calculating Size Table.                 Dice= Characteristic Dice (if Str = 2D, Dice = 2).
                                 Total the dice used to generate the Physical Character-                               Total     = C1 + C2 + C3
                              istics (half-Grace, half-Agility, and half-Vigor; double-Stami-                   Typical Size     = Total * 12
                              na). Multiply the total by 12 (based on an average 3.5 per die         For example, Human SDEIES rolls 6D (2D +2D +2D) for
                              and approximately 3.4 kg of flesh per Physical Characteristic        C1 C2 C3 physical characteristics. Size = 6 *12 =72. A Hu-
                              point 3.5 x 3.4= 12). The result is typical size for the sophont.    man is size 72 (=about 72 liters volume and 72 kg weight).
                                    For example, a Virushi rolls Str= 5D Dex= 3D End= 2D
                              for a total of 10D. Because Str is 4D or greater, multiply 10D
                              by 120 = 1200 liters = 1200 kilograms (= a little over a ton).
                                                                                                   B BODY FORM PROFILE
                                                                                                   BFP Width Depth Descriptor
                                                                                                     1    L/1     L/1     Cubic. Max volume
                              Calculating Size (Height and Weight)
                                                                                                     1.4 L/1.4 L/1.4      Globular. Ball. Orb
                                 Physical height and weight of a character can be deter-
                                                                                                     2    L/ 2 L/ 2
                              mined from the physical characteristics C1 C2 C3.
                                                                                                     3    L/ 3 L/ 3       Tortoise
                                 Body Form Profile BFP equates known animal struc-
                                                                                                     4    L/ 4 L/ 4
                              tures with a uniform cylinder allowing calculation of volume,
                                                                                                     5    L/ 5 L/ 5       Hippo, Bison
                              mass, and hit points (and UPP characteristic points) based
                                                                                                     6    L/ 6 L/ 6       Primate
                              on Length. BFP=1 is a cube. BFP=1.4 is a sphere.
                                                                                                     7    L/ 7 L/ 7       Vthick. Obese Human
                                 BFP= 9 approximates a Human figure.
                                                                                                     8    L/ 8 L/ 8       Thick. Heavy Human
                                 L W D can be transposed after calculation: adopt Depth
                                                                                                     9    L/ 9 L/ 9       Typical Human
                              as Height and Length as Depth. A Hexaphant could be 0.5 m
                                                                                                    10    L /10 L /10     Thin Human
                              tall, and wide, and 2.6 m long: a sort of long mini-elephant.
                                                                                                    11    L /11   L /11   Vthin. Ostrich
                                 Using The BFP Table for Volume and Mass. For a stat-
                                                                                                    12    L /12 L /12
                              ed L Length (the maximum Length, or the Height if upright)
                                                                                                    13    L /13 L /13
                              in meters, W Width (= L / BFP) and D Depth (= L / BFP).
                                                                                                    14    L /14 L /14     Crocodile
                              Multiply L x W x D for the volume in m3. Multiply by 1000 for
                                                                                                    15    L /15 L /15
                              volume in liters and mass in kilograms.
                                                                                                    16    L /16 L /16     T Rex
                                 For example, a 1.8 meter (6 foot) tall human (BFP=9) is
                                                                                                    20    L /20 L /20     Shark
                              L=1.8, W= 1.8 /9 = 0.2, D= 1.8 /9 = 0.2 L xW xD = 1.8 x 0.2 x
                                                                                                    25    L /24 L /24     Anaconda
                              0.2 = 0.072 m3 = 72 liters = 72 kilograms= Size 72.

                                 Using The BFP Table for Length. Using Sophont Size
                                                                                                   100    L/100 L /100 Sheet. Leaf. Film
                              and BFP, find L Length in meters. Convert Size (/1000) to
                              Volume in m3. Multiply Volume by BFP squared and find the              BFP= Body Form Pro le Divisor.
                              cube root.                                                             L= Length. W= Width. D= Depth. Note that Width and
                                 A 72 kilogram Human (BFP=9) is Size=72. Convert Size              Depth are average over body length rather than maximum.
                              to Volume (=72/1000 =) 0.072. Multiply by BFP Squared
                              (=0.072* 9*9=) 5.8. Find the cube root = 1.79 meters tall.
                              The Human is about 1.8 meters tall.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                                             1
                                                                                                                  Sophont Creation

                   Calculate Height or Length of a Sophont based on its Size or Bulk.
                   Columns are labelled with Body Form Profile. Using the Size (or
                 Bulk) row, find the Body Form Profile column. The intersection provides
                 Sophont Height or Length in meters.
                                                                                                         Sophont

                Using The Sophont Height and Sophont Bulk Tables
                   Assume a sophont BFP=9 and 200 cm (2 meters) tall. On D Sophont Height in Column 9, locate 2.0 and read the row identi-
                fier for Size= 96. Such an individual is generated with 8D and so probably has Str=3D and Dex or End =3D.
                   Assume a sophont roughly ball-shaped BFP= 1.4 and 1,000 kilograms in mass. On E Sophont Bulk, locate the row cor-
                responding to 1,000 kg (=1020) and the column corresponding to 1.4 (midway between columns 1 and 2. The sophont has a
                height (diameter) roughly 1.3 meters.
                   The Virushi weighs about a ton. Based on an image, it is about 3 meters long. Interpolation on E Sophont Bulk indicates
                BFP=5, which confirms the image.

                  16             Sophont
                                 Uniques
                                                                           Sophonts may have unusual, unique, or non-standard physical or
                                                                       life cycle structures. These uniques may be implemented for specific
                                                                       sophonts as desired or appropriate. This list is not exhaustive.
                                                                           This system cannot cover every possible situation (indeed, no sys-
                tem can). Where necessary, or where desired, truly unique elements of the Sophont structure can be inserted. Uniques may
                change, supplement, complement, or supersede any of the other sophont information.
                  Reasons. Uniques are best imposed when they have a game-play or background reason supporting the specific concept.
                The Tinroa of Shvireeyiyi experience an aging crisis at Life Stage 5. The Intferhi of Beta Hydrae have two heads.

                SYMBIONTS                                                         METAMORPHOSIS
                      Symbionts are organisms that live in paired relation-            Metamorphosis is an abrupt and dramatic change in the

                nated by and controlled by a Rider. This implies (but is not      phont changes from its First Form to its Ultimate Form.
                certain) that the Rider has greater Intelligence.                      Complex Metamorphosis. Metamorphoses occurs

                MOLT                                                              specific gender or caste.
                     The sophont sheds its outer covering in an abrupt pro-
                cess at the end of a Life Stage. When Molt is complete (usu-
                ally a matter of a day or two) the subject emerges with a
                fresh outer covering (perhaps with changed markings and
                color) and may have changes in physical and even mental
                characteristics. Multiple Molts are possible (Second Molt,
                Third Molt, Fourth Molt and beyond).
                     Incremental. One or more of the Physical [and Mental]
                Characteristics increments higher.
                     Random. One or more of the Physical [and Mental)
                Characteristics changes higher or lower.

                 BWAPS                                                            DROYNE




                   The Bwaps of Marhaban (Glowl-2 [G4 V]) A5697AB-D               The Droyne of Droynia (original location unknown)

                 VIRUSHI




                                                      The Virushi of Virshash (Thintle-6 [F9 V] DA86954-6
                     The Virushi are among the largest intelligent species encountered by Humaniti. Formidable in appearance, they are
                 gentle giants with a core philosophy that respects all life as an essential part of the universe. Those who travel beyond Vir-
                 shash do so in pursuit of that philosophy, usually as doctors dedicated to the healing arts.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                                                    1
                                                                                                      Sophont Creation Examples
                 URSAE




                                                       The Ursae of Terra (Sol-3 [G2 V] A867A69-F)
                     The Ursae are uplifted bears produced by genetic experiments during the Interstellar Wars; later attempts to terminate
                 the population scattered small groups to remote systems. Ursa Minor are roughly Human-sized and derived from Black
                 Bears; Ursa Major are derived from Grizzly Bears.

                 DANDELIONS                                                        THE BLACK ANTS




                 The Llellewyloly of Junidy (Lhininin-5 [F7 V]) B434ABD-B)                                The Myrmidons
                      The Llellewyloly (commonly: Dandelions) have five                 Myrmidons (commonly: Black Ants) appear occasion-
                 multi-jointed multi-function (hands, feet, senses) limbs; the     ally (and unpredictably) as small independent colonies de-
                 central body is covered with long, coarse hair.                   voted to exploitation of resources. Their origins are obscure.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                              - 246 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                                                                                                  BeastMaker
                                  Life abounds in the universe, and travellers may encounter many different na-
                              tive life forms as they travel. Animals [Beasts] in any ecological system interact
                              with each other by forming food chains, obeying instincts, defending territory, and
                              generally living out their lives. Travellers naturally encounter animals.
                     Every Terrain Hex (recall that a Terrain Hex, with a diameter of 100 km, is part of a World Hex, and contains many Local
                Hexes) which can support animal life has an associated Beast Encounter Table. Adventurers consult the table when hunting,
                touring, researching, and sometimes when directed to by events or chance. Every world has a unique ecological system (or
                more than one such system), but those systems can be constructed by following a consistent set of rules.
                     MOARN Make Only As Really Necessary. Information about animals can be created quickly and easily; it is not neces-
                sary to build extensive Beast Encounter Tables in advance. Create tables only as required: if the referee knows a specific
                beast will be required, then it should be generated.
                     It may be useful to have several pre-generated encounter tables on hand for common terrain types. Even when BETs are
                pre-generated, special details can wait until absolutely needed.
                     Animal or Beast? In general, the terms are interchangeable (and are used interchangeably): an animal is a beast; a
                beast is an animal. Animal implies a mobile non-intelligent living creature; beast implies a degree of danger. We also use beast
                within the text to include swimming and flying creatures.

                ENCOUNTERING BEASTS                                                 an encounter may occur. When it does, the Beast Encounter

                      It just jumped on something. It looks like a pouncer.         THE CONCEPT OF ECOLOGICAL NICHE
                                                                                         Beasts are identified (and classified) in terms of the eco-
                     Quantity approximates the number of beasts present.            logical niches they occupy. Each beast occupies a specific
                     Size indicates a general notion of the size of the beasts.     position in the hierarchical food chain.
                Beast size is expressed on the standard Size chart and typi-             A beast that eats grass occupies a niche in the environ-
                cally ranges from 1 to 6. Some animals may be as large as           ment; an animal that eats grazers is higher in the food chain
                7 or 8. Some animals may be less than 1 (Size= T or Size=           and occupies a different (and equally essential) niche.
                S). Because Size correlates to R= Ranges and the Senses,                 Although each niche is usually filled by only one spe-
                it automatically indicates the maximum distance at which it         cies, many different species are capable of filling that niche.
                can be seen with normal vision.
                     For example, normal human vision cannot see a Size=3           LOCOMOTION
                beast beyond R=3 (about 150 meters).                                    The manner in which a beast moves is fundamental to
                     SpeedC is the expected speed of the beast on the Ve-           an understanding of its activities and behavior.
                hicle Speed table. Speed for beast encounters is further dif-
                ferentiated.                                                        Types of Locomotion

                 SpeedAF (Attack/Flee). Speed of attack and ight. An animal attacks (or when it ees) at this speed.
                 SpeedC (Conservative). Ordinary, energy-conserving speed. An animal, not attacking or eeing, uses this speed.
                 SpeedB (Burst). Short term maximum speed, equivalent to sprinting.
                 Endurance. An animal is capable of maintaining speed based on its Animal Endurance.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                and other flight mechanisms.
                     Drifts. The beast is moved by outside forces such as
                air currents (including winds, storms, and convection), water
                currents (including streams and rivers, convection, or flood-
                ing), or natural movement of the environment.
                     Static. The beast does not normally move.

                Multiple Locomotion Types
                      A beast may have multiple types of locomotion.
                      Amphibian. The beast is capable of moving on land
                and in water. It can walks and swim. For example, a frog or
                a crocodile.
                      Aquatic. The beast operates in a water environment. It
                can swim and dive. For example, a whale.
                      Flyer. Theoretically, flyer would apply to any beast
                which flies exclusively. In practice, a flyer is assumed to be
                able to walk and fly. For example, a locust, or a wren.
                      Triphibian. The beast can move on land, in water, and
                can fly. For example, a duck.
                      Flyphib. The beast moves in both water and atmo-
                sphere. It can swim and fly: a flying fish.
                      Drifter. The beast drifts (or moves based on enviromen-       larger beast. Sometimes an intermittent will attack in order to
                tal factors such as wind or current).                               protect territory or young. Typical Terran intermittents are the
                      Static. The beast is immobile.                                chipmunk and the elephant.

                tables) it appears to be a Bilateral HBS-T-LL-LL-T. It has a          appropriate as the Delphi domain has long been a champion
                bony interior skeleton, ichor as an interior fluid, a skin of fine    of peaceful coexistence. It appears to be a Bilateral HBS-T-
                scales, and may use its hooves as weapons.                            WN-LN-T. It has a cartilege interior skeleton, blood, and a
                                                                                      feathery pelt; it may use its mouth (beak) as a weapon.

                Computing Volume Using Profile and Size
                      Using Size as the greatest dimension (Length) for an object (or beast), divide Size by Divisor to determine average Width
                and average Depth. Multiply the three Length Width Depth to determine volume. Assuming an average density equal to water,
                divide the result for volume in liters and weight/ mass in kilograms.
                      Divisor produces “average” Width and Depth; use double that value for the probable dimensions of the core body. For
                example, a typical human 1.5 meters tall (Divisor= 9) has an average Depth and average Width = 0.16 meters. Maximum body
                Depth is closer to 0.33 meters (= 2x the average body Depth).
                      For example, a smallish human 1.5 meters tall (Divisor= 9) has a volume = 1.5m x 0.16m x 0.16m = 0.0384 cubic meters
                = 38 liters = 38 kilograms = 85 pounds.
                      For example a typical human 1.8 meters tall (Divisor= 9) has a volume = 1.8 m x 0.2m x 0.2 m = 0.072 cubic meters =
                72 liters = 72 kg = 158 pounds. A fat human 1.8 meters tall (Divisor = 7) has a volume = 0.119 cubic meters = 119 liters = 119
                kg = 262 pounds. An obese human 1.8 meters tall (Divisor=6) has a volume = 0.162 cubic meters = 162 liters = 162 kg = 356
                pounds.

                                          02               Body Structure
                                                                                                                 Physical appearance is determined by body symmetry,
                                                                                                              number of limb groups, location of the braincase and senses,
                                                                                                              armor status of the body, natural weaponry, and other details.

                                                A. Body Structure Elements                                               torso, limbs, and tail in the format:
                                                Symmetry. Bilateral= paired; Trilateral= triplets; Radial = 1D limbs

                                          per group. Asymmetrical= 1D limbs per group (roll for each limb group).

                                                Tails. Roll for the presence of a Tail. Manipulator is a prehensile
                                          tail. Proboscis is technically not a tail; it is a Trunk emanating from the

                    Table shows the largest dimension for the object. Size re ects the greatest dimension of an object or an animal. Pro le
                 determines its other dimensions: width and depth, and allows the computation of other information (such as weight / mass).


                 BP BEASTPOWER                              Steady BP. For long       E ANIMAL ENDURANCE
                    Beastpower is a standardized measure term  work, Speed=1
                                                                                      = time (in hours) animal can move at Speed=1
                 of the power of animals and vehicles.   (thus BP= mass tons).

                 04                   Details                                       BEAST TRAINING
                                                                                        Some beasts may be susceptible to training based on
                                                                                    three basic tasks: Defer, Obey, and Learn Simple Task.
                                                                                        Defer. The beast accepts the trainer as a superior com-
                                                                                    panion. It will not attack unless provoked.
                FOOD FOR SURVIVAL                                                       Obey. The beast accepts the trainer as its master. It will
                     Animals may provide food for subsistence and survival.         engage in training situations.
                     Personal Food Requirements. An individual requires                 Learn Simple Task. The beast learns a basic or simple

                grams of meat and about 250 total grams of food; an active          responses are built on a series of simple tasks.
                person require a minimum 4 of them per day).
                     Animal Food Yield. An Edible Animal provides food/             Training Beasts
                meat= one-half total animal weight.                                       Many beasts are unable to respond to the three basic
                     A Marginal Edible Animal provides food/meat= one-              tasks. Each species has its own response to the three tasks.
                quarter total animal weight.                                              Trainability. On the Beast Training Difficulty Table, roll

                per kilogram of animal weight).                                     in all three tasks).

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                BeastMaker Events
                                 The unusual is always a challenge to travellers. The unforeseen or the extraor-
                              dinary event can hinder or help any journey.
                     Events are an important part of encounter tables. They force detours and delays; they provide insights into local terrain,
                and they challenge the talents and resources of travelers.
                     An event is a non-animal encounter within a terrain type. Most events are temporary or transient effects which, when the
                terrain is revisited, may no longer be in present. Some events are permanent (and are reasonably called terrain features).
                     For example, a flooded path is an event; it is temporary and may not be encountered the next time the terrain is visited.
                A natural stone bridge is permanent terrain feature which will probably be present (absent erosion or a worldquake) when the
                terrain is next visited.
                     Events Are Diversions and Challenges. Events give an interesting dimension to travel through terrain; they are not a
                substitute for referee imposed activities directly related to play.
                     Events are not necessarily apparent or visible; they merely exist. It is entirely possible for adventurers to not notice an
                event and simply pass it by.
                     Event Size. An event is typically placed in a Single Hex within a Local Hex. Some events may extend to adjacent hexes
                or farther.

                EVENT LOCATION                                                           Special Events reflect interesting, unusual, dangerous,
                    An event (the center of the event, or its most visible part)    or valuable items or effects within the terrain. They include
                should be placed within a Local Hex.                                unusual signs of intelligence, civilization, or settlement,
                    Locate The Adventurers. Determine the location of the           whether current or past, and things or effects which may in-
                adventurers in the Local Hex.                                       fluence or direct the actions of those who visit the terrain.
                    Place the Event. Determine the location of the event. If
                necessary, extend it to other hexes.                                The Elements of the Event
                                                                                          An event can be described with a title (or descriptive
                EVENT CATEGORIES                                                    name), a size (detailing the extent of its influence), and an
                      Events can be identified by their origin within the envi-     effect (a short sentence or paragraph noting its activity and
                ronment.                                                            the effect it has on individuals and equipment).
                      Meteorologic Events reflect weather conditions, in-                 Mud. Local Hex. Surface vehicles are hindered by an
                cluding storms, extremes of heat and cold, extremes of              expanse of clinging wet soil. Speed is reduced to Crawl.
                atmospheric turbulence, and deviations from normal or ex-                 Circling Flyers: A number of flyers spot the party and
                pected weather.                                                     circle above their heads. After about 10 minutes the party
                      Atmospheric Events reflect the state of the atmo-             will be attacked by chasers. The animals are symbiotic: the
                sphere, including local variations in environmental gases           flyers spot prey for the chasers and are allowed to share in
                (and their effects), visibility, and sound transmission.            the feast.
                      Planetologic (or Geologic) Events reflect local fea-                 Poisonous Pests: While the party was stopped, ttny
                tures or anomalies of the world surface, including terrain fea-     (one gram) creatures have crawled into concealed places
                tures and barriers, and the presence or absence of minerals.        within the party’s equipment (packs, boots, etc.) They are
                      Tectonic Events reflect the influence of the world inte-      poisonous, and they attack when encountered (when a char-
                rior on the world surface, including volcanism, worldquakes,        acter reaches into his pack, puts on his boots, etc ).
                and magnetic and gravitic anomalies.                                      Stampede: A herd of grazers, frightened by carnivores,
                      Hydrologic Events reflect local features and anomalies        stampedes into the party. They can be turned by loud noises
                of water or environmental liquids, including floods, tides, riv-    (gunshots, explosions) or laser bolts. Otherwise, they will run
                ers and bodies of water, and abundance or scarcity of liquids.      straight through the party.
                      Stellar Events reflect the influence of the local star (or          Animated Vines: Ordinary looking vines grab and hold
                stars) on the world surface, including levels of heat, irregu-      individuals in a constricting grip, inflicting 1D damage to Str-
                larities, flares, and eclipses.                                     Dex-End per minute. Release requires cutting the vines.

                              Beastiary
                                        Animals — beasts — abound in the universe. Every world presents new op-
                                     portunities to encounter the near infinite variety of fauna shaped by specific world
                                     environments and conditions.
                                  The following is a representative list of animals encountered in Charted Space. Some are common, some are uncommon,
                              and some are exclusive to certain worlds.

                                Speed is the attacking/fleeing speed of the animal (Speed2) and is described in terms consistent with those on the Vehicle
                              Speed Table at page 295. Thus, an Anola is indeed “XSlow” (1 band per turn) relative to an automobile. For ease, the referee
                              may use the alternate terms (e.g. XSlow is also a Human “Sprint”) and include the speed in bands per turn after the word.
                              Pouncers should be marked with an asterisk as a reminder that its Pounce may occur at SpeedB

                                                                                               ANOLA
                                                                                                     Length: 0.75m. Volume: 2.9L. Burden: 1 kg. End: 6.
                                                                                                     An arboreal omnivore native to Pysadi Spin 3008
                                                                                               C5766D8-5, occurring nowhere else in the wild or in cap-
                                                                                               tivity. Anolas are revered as holy by the Mother Church of
                                                                                               Pysadi. Their export, capture, or study by any but those high
                                                                                               in the religious hierarchy is banned. They are kept in spe-
                                                                                               cial, heavily-guarded garden preserves and are cared for by
                                                                                               specially appointed keepers. Killing one of these animals is
                                                                                               a capital offense. Although anolas are occasionally encoun-
                                                                                               tered in the wild on Pysadi, those close to civilized areas
                                                                                               have been taken into the preserves.
                                                                                                     Anolas mass an average of 3 kilograms, and are usu-
                                                                                               ally between 50 and 75 centimeters in length, including their
                                                                                               two prehensile tails. Since anolas were known only from
                                                                                               poorly preserved pelts smuggled off-planet, for many years
                                                                                               there were thought to be three species, but as more com-
                                                                                               plete specimens became available, it was determined that
                                                                                               the three different types were only different sexual phases of
                                                                                               one species. Respiration is accomplished by paired lungs in
                                                        Anola                                  the upper chest cavity. The circulatory system is closed, with
                                                                                        - 262 -

                a pair of two chambered hearts moving the blood. Gas ex-
                change is typical, making use of an iron-based hemoglobin.
                Details of the neuro-muscular system and the digestive sys-
                tem are not presently available. Anolas are now known to be
                hermaphroditic, and the three phases are stages in sexual
                development. Upon reaching maturity, an anola’s male sys-
                tem becomes active, the female system remaining dormant.
                Under certain conditions, the male system degenerates,
                glands for the nourishment of the young develop, and the
                male becomes a neuter, or parental. Under specific alternate
                conditions, the female system of a parental will activate, and
                the individual becomes a female. Little is known for certain
                about the details of the reproductive cycle, but the following
                is currently the accepted sequence of events.                                                  Beaker

                     Length: 0.75m. Volume: 2.9L. Burden: 0.9 kg. Endur-          are bipedal, using a muscular pair of hind legs for locomo-

                     Beakers (as they are often called) weigh from 2 to 3
                kilograms, and measure 60 to 75 cm in length, half of which
                is generally tail (tails are sometimes bobbed on animals kept
                aboard spacecraft). Beakers are covered by a short fur, most
                commonly brown or gray. Black is rare, and white extremely
                so. The skeleton and musculature follow typical Terran ver-
                tebrate norms. Respiration is accomplished by the usual
                paired lung arrangement, the circulatory system is closed
                and the heart four-chambered, making the animal physically
                very similar to the Terran squirrel monkey.
                     The animal’s most notable feature, the beak, is formed of                             Bush Runner
                two bony projections from the palate and mandible, covered
                                                                             - 263 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                     The head is a typical arrangement of brain surrounded
                by a bony cranium upon which are laterally paired sensory
                organs (eyes, nose and ears), as well as a ventrally located
                mouth. The teeth are arranged in common fashion for omni-
                vores (dental formula 2-1-2-2) and are faced on their grind-
                ing surfaces with a silicate material rather than the calcium
                enamel of Terran vertebrates.
                     Respiration is the common O2/CO2 exchange accom-
                plished by paired lungs located in the upper body cavity. The
                circulatory system is closed, the heart is four-chambered
                and the blood gases are transported by a copper based he-
                moglobin, which make the blood blue in color.

                      Groats have a secret weapon; a small gland under the
                tail can expel a noxious fluid to a distance of nearly 3m. Like
                pepper or tear gas, it makes a victim concentrate on flight
                rather than attack. Strangely, this fluid is ineffective against
                their most common predator: the leaping snowcat.
                      At irregular times, groats migrate from the mountain
                meadows to desert oases for mating and for the old ones to
                die. The males fight for control of the herd, after which the
                herd returns home.

                bat between males before mating.
                     Due to their hardy nature, kian were exported as beasts
                of burden, and are common in the coreward Imperium, both
                domesticated and in the wild. The sturdiness of their overall
                frames has made them a frequent choice for use as mounts
                and pack animals. Kian can carry up to 250kg comfortably,
                although they will refuse to move if overloaded. They cannot
                tolerate thin atmospheres, and require a special filter/muzzle
                (Cr50) for tainted atmospheres.

                toria, the bones of Kudebeck’s gazelle are the only viable
                metal substitute. Without the kudies, complex mechanical
                devices such as the native dirigibles upon which planetary
                commerce depends, could not be constructed.
                     Kudies inhabit the upland valleys of Victoria, where the
                sufficient vegetation supports them; rough ground provides
                cover from predators. The six limbs give a slight advantage
                on rough slopes as they are able to use four legs for footing,
                and two for defense. The illustration is of a female in a typical
                fighting posture. Kudies are usually a dark grey color on the
                back and sides, fading to a lighter shade on the underside.

                REMEX MUTA (DUMB PULLER)
                     Length: 5m. Volume: 14 m3 (fat); 5 m3 (lean). Burden:
                4500. Endurance: 6.
                     The early explorers of the Vilani Empire, despite their
                powerful interstellar drives, were handicapped with inef-
                ficient power supplies. Once they arrived at a planet, their
                vehicles were restricted by available fuel. Like so many fron-
                tiers before them, they turned to beasts of burden.
                     On Anzalits Vlan 2820 EA9A210-9 they found the near
                perfect beast of burden: remex muta, or the dumb puller.                                    Dumb Puller
                     The sixped amphibious remex was shaped by the un-
                                                                              - 269 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Adventures
                                  Adventure is the term for activities undertaken by characters as they live their
                              lives. Enjoyable adventures satisfy the needs of the players and transform the
                              adventure’s story into a unique experience with respect to specific characters and
                              their players.

                     Traveller adventures span the entire range of experience that can be expected in the universe of the far future. The po-
                tential for adventure is endless, depending as it does on the situation and on the characters themselves. Nevertheless, each
                adventure can be classified in a number of different ways. These classification schemes can help any referee to produce his
                or her own adventures.
                     Traveller players are always in search of adventure. Some adventure comes from playing the game system, from design-
                ing starships, and from exploring worlds generated by the referee. More adventure comes from the scenarios that the referee
                supervises, and from participating in campaigns. The interaction of the characters involved, the imaginations of all the players,
                and the details of the game system make the entire game fun for hours on end.

                TYPES OF ADVENTURES                                                known situation where they must find information about their
                     Adventures can be classified by their patrons and situ-       environment either to ensure their own survival or as part of
                ations. They may have a variety of settings and catalysts.         some interest they have.
                                                                                        Enrichment makes economic, social, intellectual, or
                Patrons                                                            other improvement the primary goal. Such adventures are
                     are non-player characters who provide direction and           mercenary (although not necessarily military) in nature.
                guidance to the players. In many cases, they speak with the             Enigma/mystery presents a situation for the characters
                voice of the referee in providing their help.                      to solve. It may be a simple murder mystery, with clues all
                     A Patron Mission usually involves a Noun and a Verb.          around, or it may be a puzzling alien structure about which
                The noun is an object (construed liberally to include things,      the group is curious.
                people, animals, and even information) and the verb is an               Novelty adventures place the characters in interesting
                action (which can include any conceivable activity). Adjec-        situations and allow them to deal with them. A visit to an in-
                tives are sprinkled liberally to add detail.                       terstellar casino for a round of gambling is a novelty.
                     Some examples include:
                     Find Rare Animal. Recover Lost Book. Carry Important          Settings
                Message. Protect Vital Secret. Return Stolen Gem. Discover              are locations for adventures. The setting may remain
                Important Fact. Locate Lost Starship. Guard Valuable Cargo.        the same, but because most adventures involve travel, they
                     With time and a growing knowledge of the universe, the        usually vary. Three basic settings for adventures are the
                players themselves will develop their own missions and be-         ship, the outside location, and the enclosed location.
                come for a time, their own patrons.                                     The ship covers any vessel, whether marine, interplan-
                     Rumors. A Patron need not be a person: news items,            etary or interstellar, or other type; ships provide interesting
                encyclopedia entries, and even rumors may be enough to             movable settings with abundances of machinery and other
                motivate the players to undertake a mission.                       equipment. The ship location also extends to many vehicles.
                                                                                        The outside location is geographic in nature: it is usu-
                Situations                                                         ally a world surface hex of some specific size.
                     indicate the actual nature of the adventure: the probable          The enclosed location indicates any building or natu-
                actions the players are expected to take. Situations include:      ral feature and is usually indoors; it is often presented as a
                     Chase/pursuit may involve characters on either side of        maze or labyrinth to be explored and conquered.
                the situation, and it is possible for events to turn the tables
                on the players, converting the pursuers to the pursued on a        Catalysts
                moment’s notice.                                                        provide interest in an adventure. They include danger
                     Assault/rescue usually involves force or violence in          (which forces action through threats), opportunity (which
                overwhelming enemy characters or the forces of nature in           forces action through a promise of reward), and puzzles
                order to obtain some goal. Characters may be on either side        (which prompt action through curiosity). By assembling these
                of the assault/rescue.                                             aspects of adventures together, the referee can produce in-

                                                                               - 272 -




Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                The Pull
                     The pull is a simple name for a goal that attracts adven-
                turers, much like a magnet attracts iron. It can be as simple
                as a fabled mineral deposit on a distant world, or as complex
                as a secret formula that will keep the sun from going nova—
                to be found within a certain time limit.
                     Pulls must be tailored to players in the adventure. For
                an anthropologist, his pull can be the secret of some sophont
                species that disappeared millenia ago; for a soldier, the pull
                can be a long-sought bit of training from a military society,
                available only after he has proven his worth; for a craftsman,
                it may be that final piece of perfectly forged metal with which
                he can complete his latest masterpiece.
                     An adventure can have many pulls: one major and sev-
                eral minor. The major pull is the ultimate goal of the action,
                but the minor pulls continue to motivate the individual char-
                acters. Shifting emphasis can make the overall adventure
                realistic; a realistic course for the action is rarely a straight-                      Alexander Jamison
                forward path directly to the adventurer’s seeming goal.                         and the crew of Free Trader Beowulf
                                                                                                                   in
                The Push                                                                                  Canicus Run

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                EPIC Adventures
                                  Adventures should be fun. Enjoyable adventures satisfy the needs of the play-
                              ers and transform the adventure’s story into an experience unique for the specific
                              characters and their players. Enjoyable adventures happen when everyone is fa-
                              miliar with the game rules, and rules resolution proceeds easily without interrupting
                              the flow of events.
                      Traveller adventures can be a simple string of encounters and actions set against a pre-generated background as players
                fly from world to world, engaging in trade and speculation, seeking and finding patrons, taking on and solving problems, and
                generally wandering about the universe. Such adventures within the Traveller universe can be challenging and rewarding.
                      On the other hand, with a small bit of effort by the referee, an adventure can be taken to the next level of interest and
                excitement. The EPIC Adventure System makes use of the talents of the referee to conduct an adventure set against exist-
                ing or provided background material.


                                                E                    P                      I                   C
                                         Easy:                Playable:            Interactive:          Checklist:
                                         Implemented with     Oriented to situa-   Responsive to         Simple, easy to
                                         minimum effort by    tions which reveal   the interests,        use Player and
                                         the Referee and      information and      goals, whims          Referee record-
                                         no new playing       allow its use to     and needs of the      keeping for all
                                         techniques for the   move the game        Players and the       aspects of the
                                         players.             events forward.      Referee.              Adventures.
                      The ultimate intended result is a structured adventure that is interesting, not necessarily predictable, and (above all) fun.

                 A               Creating
                                 EPIC Adventures
                     The Epic Creation Process is undertaken in six steps:
                     A. The Synopsis. Create a full disclosure synopsis (for
                the referee) of what the adventure includes.                        B Once Upon A Time In The Future
                     B. Acts and Scenes. Create short scene descriptions.
                     C. Background. Write one or more encyclopedic data
                                                                                         1 Waypoints In The Solar System
                entries supporting each Scene. Entries may include maps,                A   Visit the Solar Power Fields of Mercury
                diagrams, world or location data, and other information.                B   Visit the Terraforming Satellite Orbiting Venus      Act 1
                     D. Organize Data. Organize the data entries. Cross ref-            C   Visit the Subarean Warrens below Olympus
                erence each Scene with data entries. Create additional data
                                                                                        D   Visit Prospector Central on Ceres
                entries (without direct connections to the Epic).
                     E. Test, Proof, and Revise. Analyze the Epic to ensure             E   Visit the UN Outpost on Io
                that it flows properly, makes sense when played, and pro-
                vides sufficient interest to potential players.                             2 Encounters
                     F. Create Final Checklist. Create the final checklist              A   Encounter Professor Huerta
                with associated Synopsis and data entries.                              B   Encounter Space Patrol Captain John Jamison          Act 2

                                                                                                                         EPIC Adventures

                                                                                                         Themes in                                     1
                      Theme is the unifying subject or idea within the adventure.
                It is often expressed as a generalization.
                                                                                                        Adventures                 Th
                      In literature, the author is driven by a universally applicable idea or subject. The events, plot, and characters reflect this
                underlying theme.
                      In an EPIC adventure, the referee administers the events, plot, and character encounters while considering the influence
                of the theme. The theme constantly guides and inspires the referee.
                      Random Theme. Before the adventure begins, the referee consults the table for the theme for the current session.
                      A Thread of Themes. For a series of adventures, a well-thought-out thread of themes can be used; the thread builds in
                importance as the series moves toward its conclusion.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                Crimes and Punishments
                                   Crime is the violation of law serious enough to be punished by the government.
                              It is additionally classified by three distinct identifiers: type (or category), degree
                              (or intensity), and intention.
                      There are many types of law. Two are of special importance: civil and criminal.
                      Civil Law specifies the details of society and commerce: contracts and agreements, relationships (nobility; marriage and
                child-raising rights and obligations, corporations and organizations), and the law of wrongs (torts: wrongs that merit compen-
                sation rather than punishment).
                      Criminal Law governs violations serious enough to merit personal consequences: deliberate infliction of wrongs, violation
                of the standard or customary laws, and serious violation of social expectations. Civil and criminal law often overlap: civil law
                may expect a female to have two spouses; criminal law may make the violation serious enough to be punished.
                      There is a third type of law: Social law (or Cultural law). Social law governs the basic structure and expectations that
                society has for its members, and includes custom, etiquette and politeness, and taboo.
                      Crime – misconduct which society will punish – varies from culture to culture and from world to world. One society may
                punish almost nothing. On some worlds, certain modes of dress may be criminal; on others specific forms of expression may
                be illegal. Nevertheless, there is a consistency to the definition of illegal activity even across the vast distances of space.

                THE CATEGORIES OF CRIME                                             pests; individuals refusing to own such predators may be

                Important Terms and Concepts
                                 Traveller uses certain words and abbreviations in a unique manner, and in
                              order to be able to properly understand the Traveller rules, players and referees
                              should acquire some familiarity with these terms.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                                       THE MILLENNIA OF STAR TRAVEL
                                 In very broad terms, modern interstellar history
                              can be divided into thousand-year Millennia, each
                              with a distinct character.
                                 The First Millennium of Star Travel (-9000 to
                              -8000). Following the discover of basic Jump Drive,
                              the Humans of Vland expand to dominate interstellar
                              travel and trade.
                                 The Second Millennium of Star Travel (-8000 to
                              -7000). The Vilani continue to exploit their interstel-
                              lar drive.
                                 The Third Millennium of Star Travel (-7000 to
                              -6000). The Vilani suppress the aspirations of the
                              other intelligent species they encounter, including
                              through significant violent means.
                                 The Fourth Millennium of Star Travel (-6000 to
                              -5000). Just as subject species begin to organize
                              their resistance, the Vilani discover the improved
                              double speed Jump-2 Drive. This provides the sig-
                              nificant advantage needed to consolidate their hold
                              on their subject worlds. Vilani rule is formalized as
                              the Ziru Sirka, the Grand Empire of Stars.
                                 The Fifth Millennium of Star Travel (-5000 to
                              -4000). To maintain power and order, technology is
                              suppressed; the attention of subject world popula-
                              tions is diverted to social and recreational pursuits.
                                 The Sixth Millennium of Star Travel (-4000 to
                              -3000). The Ziru Sirka becomes brutal in its efforts
                              to maintain power.
                                 The Seventh Millennium of Star Travel (-3000 to
                              -2000). The Humans of Terra independently develop
                              Jump Drive and, not knowing any better, challenge
                              the far larger First Imperium in a series of Interstellar
                              Wars that ultimately lead to a Vilani collapse.
                                 The Eighth Millennium of Star Travel (-2000 to
                              -1000). Terra and its hundred worlds attempts to rule
                              the ten-thousand-world Vilani Empire as a captured
                              territory. However short-lived, The Rule Of Man nev-
                              ertheless imposed Terran (and Anglic-language) so-
                              ciety on many Vilani worlds, but ultimately collapsed
                              into chaos.
                                 The Ninth Millennium of Star Travel (-1000 to
                              0). The demise of The Rule Of Man leads to the Long
                              Night which isolates worlds. Many die back to bar-
                              renness; others regress in technology.
                                 The Tenth Millennium of Star Travel (0 to 1000).
                              The Sylean Federation re-established the Vilani Em-
                              pire as the Third Imperium and expands to re-absorb
                              many of its worlds.

Mark Ayers (Order #7356100)
                          Previous editions of the Traveller role-playing game system:
                                      Classic Traveller (CT). The original edition of Traveller published by GDW Game Designers’ Work-

                         1            shop 1977 and revised in 1981. The intention was a generic science- ction system, but it quickly
                                      concentrated on the Third Imperium as a setting supported with adventures and supplements.
                                      MegaTraveller (MT). The second edition of Traveller published by GDW 1987, introduced a uni ed

                         2            task game mechanic supported by a fully developed skill system; it advanced the Traveller universe
                                      history and setting into the Rebellion era.


                         3
                                      Traveller: The New Era (TNE). The third edition of Traveller, the last to be published by GDW
                                      1993, adopted GDW’s RPG House System rules (also used for Twilight: 2000 and Dark Conspira-
                                      cy). Adventures chronicled the aftermath of a widespread collapse of interstellar civilization.
                                      Marc Miller’s Traveller (T4). The fourth edition of Traveller, published by Imperium Games 1996,

                         4            (after GDW closed its doors). Its adventures and supplements chronicle the founding years (begin-
                                      ning in Year Zero) of the Third Imperium.
                                      GURPS Traveller (GT). A parallel edition (published by Steve Jackson Games 1998) chronicling an

                         9            alternate universe in which Strephon was not assassinated and the MegaTraveller Rebellion did
                                      not happen. This edition adapted the setting to the Steve Jackson Games GURPS rules set.


                         7
                                      Traveller20 (T20) An adaptation of Traveller (published by Quiklink Interactive 2002) to the popular
                                      d20 system. Its adventures and supplements chronicle the period of the Solomani Rim War and
                                      focus on the Gateway Domain of the Third Imperium.
                                      Traveller HERO (TH). An adaptation of the role-playing rules from Hero Games. Materials provide

                         =            details of characters, starships, and other equipment translated to the Hero System from various
                                      other editions.
                                      Mongoose Traveller (MgT). Produced as a universal science- ction role-playing rules set ( rst

                         8            published by Mongoose Games 2007), this edition again emphasized the Spinward Marches and its
                                      surrounding sectors.
                        This edition of Traveller:
                                      Traveller5 (T5). The fth of the direct line of editions of the Traveller game system, ambitiously

                         5            intended as the ultimate science- ction role-playing system covering near everything in role-playing,
                                      and capable of managing situations across a variety of eras and technology levels.
